questions.stem: Which is the correct ascending order of Bloom's Revised Taxonomy?

options.A: Remember→Understand→Apply→Analyse→Evaluate→Create
options.B: Understand→Remember→Apply→Create→Analyse→Evaluate
options.C: Remember→Apply→Understand→Evaluate→Analyse→Create
options.D: Apply→Remember→Understand→Analyse→Create→Evaluate

questions.explanation: Anderson & Krathwohl (2001) revised the taxonomy into six ascending cognitive levels: Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyse (L4), Evaluate (L5), Create (L6). Each level presupposes mastery of the level below.
questions.source: Anderson & Krathwohl. A Taxonomy for Learning, Teaching, and Assessing. Pearson, 2001. https://www.bloomstaxonomy.net

questions.stem: Which cognitive verbs are associated with Bloom's Level 1 (Remember)?

options.A: Critique, justify, defend, appraise
options.B: Design, construct, develop, produce
options.C: Define, identify, list, recall, state
options.D: Classify, compare, explain, interpret

questions.explanation: L1 (Remember) targets retrieval of previously learned information. Associated verbs: define, duplicate, identify, list, memorise, recall, repeat, state. These contrast with L2 verbs (explain, classify) and L3 verbs (execute, solve).
questions.source: Anderson & Krathwohl. A Taxonomy for Learning. 2001. https://www.bloomstaxonomy.net

questions.stem: 'Explain why a sorted array is required for binary search to work correctly.' At which Bloom level does this question operate?

options.A: L1 — binary search is a factual concept to memorise
options.B: L2 — requires explaining the principle connecting the sorting precondition to the algorithm's logic
options.C: L3 — requires implementing binary search in code
options.D: L4 — requires evaluating the efficiency of different search algorithms

questions.explanation: The verb 'explain' and the demand to connect a precondition (sorted order) to a mechanism (halving the search space) require understanding — Bloom's L2. No implementation (L3) or comparative evaluation (L4) is asked.
questions.source: Anderson & Krathwohl. A Taxonomy for Learning. 2001.

questions.stem: How do Bloom's L5 (Evaluate) and L6 (Create) differ in the cognitive demand they place on learners?

options.A: L5 requires memorisation; L6 requires computation
options.B: L5 requires making judgements based on criteria and evidence; L6 requires synthesising elements into a novel, coherent whole
options.C: Both require only recall from multiple sources
options.D: L5 = procedural knowledge; L6 = declarative knowledge

questions.explanation: L5 (Evaluate): learners judge, critique, or justify using standards. L6 (Create): learners produce something new by combining knowledge — the highest cognitive demand in the taxonomy.
questions.source: Anderson & Krathwohl. A Taxonomy for Learning. 2001.

questions.stem: Which of the following best exemplifies a Bloom's L3 (Apply) question on gradient descent?

options.A: Define gradient descent.
options.B: Explain how learning rate affects the convergence of gradient descent.
options.C: Trace three iterations of gradient descent on f(w)=w²−4w+5 starting at w=0 with α=0.5, showing each weight update and loss value.
options.D: Evaluate whether Adam optimiser is superior to SGD for all deep learning tasks.

questions.explanation: A=L1 (define). B=L2 (explain). D=L5 (evaluate). C requires executing the algorithm step-by-step — computing ∂L/∂w and applying w←w−α·∂L/∂w — which is L3 Apply. At w=0: ∂L/∂w=2(0)−4=−4; w₁=0−0.5×(−4)=2. At w=2: ∂L/∂w=0; converged.
questions.source: Anderson & Krathwohl. 2001; Stanford CS229. https://cs229.stanford.edu

mini_quizzes.title: MQ-2 · Memory, Forgetting, and Retrieval Practice

questions.stem: What does the Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve describe?

options.A: Relationship between study time and exam scores across a semester
options.B: Exponential decay of memory retention over time without rehearsal
options.C: Linear growth of long-term memory through repeated exposure
options.D: Cognitive load imposed by new material in a single session

questions.explanation: Ebbinghaus (1885) found retention decays exponentially without rehearsal: ~58% retained after 20 minutes, ~44% after 1 hour, ~25% after 1 week. This forms the empirical basis for spaced repetition.
questions.source: Ebbinghaus. Memory: A Contribution to Experimental Psychology. 1885. http://psychclassics.yorku.ca/Ebbinghaus/memory.htm

questions.stem: Roediger & Karpicke (2006) found which strategy produced significantly better long-term retention than re-reading?

options.A: Highlighting key passages in a textbook
options.B: Summarising notes in a different colour pen
options.C: Retrieval practice — recalling information from memory via testing
options.D: Listening to recorded lectures at 1.5× speed

questions.explanation: The testing effect: students who practised retrieval retained ~50% more material at a 1-week delayed test than those who re-studied. Retrieving strengthens the memory trace more than passive re-exposure.
questions.source: Roediger & Karpicke. Psychological Science, 17(3), 2006. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16507066

questions.stem: Why does spaced repetition produce better long-term retention than massed practice (cramming), even when total study time is equal?

options.A: Spaced repetition uses more colourful study materials
options.B: Cramming activates only short-term memory; spaced repetition is impossible to implement digitally
options.C: Each spaced retrieval resets the forgetting curve and strengthens the memory trace; massed practice benefits fade rapidly as forgetting accelerates
options.D: Spaced repetition increases the number of hours studied

questions.explanation: Each successful retrieval after a delay partially resets the forgetting curve and re-encodes memory more durably. 5×1-hour spaced sessions retain ~3× more at 2 weeks than one 5-hour massed session (Ebbinghaus, 1885; Cepeda et al., 2006).
questions.source: Ebbinghaus. 1885; Cepeda et al. Distributed practice in verbal recall tasks. Psychological Bulletin, 2006.

questions.stem: A student scores 90% immediately after a lecture but only 40% two weeks later. What does this indicate, and what is the most evidence-based intervention?

options.A: Learning disability — recommend specialist assessment
options.B: Quiz too difficult — reduce question difficulty
options.C: Normal forgetting curve decay; prescribe spaced retrieval practice sessions at day 1, 3, 7, and 14 post-learning
options.D: Student was inattentive — replay the lecture

questions.explanation: A 90%→40% drop over two weeks is consistent with Ebbinghaus's exponential forgetting curve — not a disability. The remedy: schedule review sessions at expanding intervals (1, 3, 7, 14 days) to reset the curve before severe forgetting.
questions.source: Ebbinghaus. 1885; Roediger & Karpicke. Psychological Science, 2006.

questions.stem: Designing a spaced repetition schedule for 50 Python syntax rules, 30 min/day for 4 weeks. Which schedule best applies evidence-based spacing principles?

options.A: Study all 50 rules on Day 1; spend remaining days on other subjects
options.B: Study 12–13 new rules each week in a single weekly 30-minute block
options.C: Introduce ~7 rules daily, review previous rules at expanding intervals (Day+1, +3, +7, +14) using flashcard retrieval rather than re-reading
options.D: Read all 50 rules aloud every day for 4 weeks

questions.explanation: Option C applies: (1) distributed introduction to avoid cognitive overload; (2) expanding-interval spacing to reset forgetting curves; (3) retrieval practice over passive re-reading. Options A/B mass study; D uses passive re-reading with no spacing benefit.
questions.source: Roediger & Karpicke. 2006; Cepeda et al. Psychological Bulletin, 2006.

questions.stem: In Sweller's Cognitive Load Theory, which type of cognitive load refers to the inherent difficulty of the material being learned?

options.A: Extraneous cognitive load
options.B: Germane cognitive load
options.C: Intrinsic cognitive load
options.D: Transient cognitive load

questions.explanation: Intrinsic load is determined by the complexity of the content itself — the number of interacting elements a learner must hold in working memory. Manageable by sequencing simple→complex but cannot be eliminated.
questions.source: Sweller. Cognitive Load During Problem Solving. Cognitive Science, 12(2), 1988. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15516709cog1202_4

questions.stem: Which type of cognitive load should instructional designers aim to MINIMISE?

options.A: Intrinsic load
options.B: Germane load
options.C: Extraneous load
options.D: Affective load

questions.explanation: Extraneous load is caused by poor instructional design — irrelevant information, unclear layouts, redundant explanations. It consumes working memory without contributing to schema formation and must be minimised.
questions.source: Sweller. Cognitive Science, 12(2), 1988.

questions.stem: A tutorial presents a 500-word paragraph, three unrelated code snippets, two pop-up ads, and a scrolling ticker simultaneously. Which load type is imposed and what is its effect?

options.A: Intrinsic — programming complexity fills working memory
options.B: Germane — processing irrelevant content builds useful schemas
options.C: Extraneous — poor design forces irrelevant processing, consuming working memory and reducing learning
options.D: Affective — emotional distraction from ads reduces motivation

questions.explanation: Ads, scrolling text, and unrelated snippets are irrelevant to the learning goal. Processing them imposes extraneous load — resources spent on irrelevant material reduce capacity for understanding the programming content (Sweller, 1988; Mayer, 2001).
questions.source: Sweller. 1988; Mayer. Multimedia Learning. Cambridge UP, 2001.

questions.stem: How does germane cognitive load differ from extraneous load in its effect on learning?

options.A: Both are harmful and should be minimised
options.B: Germane promotes schema construction and should be maximised; extraneous wastes working memory on irrelevant processing and should be minimised
options.C: Germane = content difficulty; extraneous = student anxiety
options.D: They are identical constructs with different names

questions.explanation: Germane load is productive cognitive effort directed at building and automating schemas — beneficial. Extraneous arises from poor design and wastes capacity. Design goal: minimise extraneous, manage intrinsic, maximise germane.
questions.source: Sweller. Cognitive Science. 1988.

questions.stem: Evaluate this question stem: 'Considering the socioeconomic, historical, technological, geopolitical, and neuroscientific contexts of ML since 1943, explain in 800 words how intrinsic load manifests when novices learn backpropagation.' Identify the primary flaw and propose a fix.

options.A: No flaw — rich multi-context maximises germane load for beginners
options.B: Excessive extraneous load: five irrelevant contexts and 800-word demand waste working memory. Fix: 'Describe two ways intrinsic load manifests when a novice first encounters backpropagation.'
options.C: Insufficient intrinsic load — too easy for advanced learners
options.D: Uses 'explain' instead of 'define' — wrong Bloom level

questions.explanation: Five contextual framing elements are extraneous — irrelevant to the core question about intrinsic load. The 800-word demand adds output pressure. The fix strips extraneous framing, uses a concise stem, and preserves the L2 cognitive demand.
questions.source: Sweller. Cognitive Science. 1988.

mini_quizzes.title: MQ-4 · Assessment Design and AI in Education

questions.stem: Which best defines formative assessment?

options.A: A high-stakes final examination counting toward a student's grade
options.B: Ongoing, low-stakes assessment used to monitor learning progress and provide feedback during instruction
options.C: A standardised national test administered once per year
options.D: A portfolio of student work collected at course end

questions.explanation: Formative assessment is assessment FOR learning — occurs during instruction, typically low-stakes, primary purpose is actionable feedback. Contrasts with summative assessment (assessment OF learning) at a course endpoint.
questions.source: Black & Wiliam. Inside the Black Box. Phi Delta Kappan, 1998; U.S. Dept. of Education. 2010. https://www2.ed.gov/rschstat/eval/tech/evidence-based-practices/finalreport.pdf

questions.stem: Bloom's 2-sigma problem (1984) found tutored students performed how many standard deviations above classroom-instructed students?

options.A: 0.5 standard deviations
options.B: 1.0 standard deviation
options.C: 2.0 standard deviations
options.D: 3.5 standard deviations

questions.explanation: Benjamin Bloom (1984) found the average tutored student outperformed 98% of conventionally instructed students — a 2 standard deviation advantage — motivating AI-driven adaptive learning at scale.
questions.source: Bloom. The 2 Sigma Problem. Educational Researcher, 13(6), 1984. https://www.jstor.org/stable/1175554

questions.stem: Why is formative assessment considered more powerful for improving learning than summative assessment alone?

options.A: Formative assessment is graded more leniently, reducing anxiety
options.B: Formative assessment provides timely, specific feedback that students can act on during learning, closing knowledge gaps before summative evaluation
options.C: Formative assessment requires less instructor preparation time
options.D: Summative assessment is only used in higher education

questions.explanation: Black & Wiliam's meta-analysis (1998): formative assessment interventions produced effect sizes of 0.4–0.7 SD — among the highest of any educational intervention. Mechanism: specific timely feedback allows correcting misconceptions while content is still being taught.
questions.source: Black & Wiliam. Inside the Black Box. Phi Delta Kappan, 80(2), 1998.

questions.stem: How does an Intelligent Tutoring System (ITS) differ from a static e-learning course in its approach to personalisation?

options.A: An ITS uses higher-resolution video
options.B: An ITS dynamically adapts content difficulty, pacing, and feedback to each learner's knowledge state; a static course delivers the same sequence to all learners
options.C: A static course is more expensive to develop
options.D: Both deliver identical experiences — only cost differs

questions.explanation: An ITS models each student's knowledge state (student model), selects the next optimal instructional action (pedagogical model), and provides immediate targeted feedback — approximating Bloom's 2-sigma tutoring effect. A static course presents a fixed sequence with no adaptation.
questions.source: VanLehn. The Relative Effectiveness of Human Tutoring, Intelligent Tutoring Systems, and Other Tutoring Systems. Educational Psychologist, 2011.

questions.stem: Designing a 5-question mini-quiz on 'Python list comprehensions' for Beginner level with a 2:2:1 Bloom ratio. Which question set is correctly structured?

options.A: 5 questions: define, state, list, name, recall — all L1
options.B: Q1: Define list comprehension (L1). Q2: Identify syntax of [x**2 for x in range(5)] (L1). Q3: Explain why comprehensions are faster than for-loops (L2). Q4: Describe a use case where a comprehension improves readability (L2). Q5: Given [1,2,3,4,5,6], write a comprehension returning only even squares (L3).
options.C: 5 questions all asking students to write code at L3
options.D: Q1–Q2: Evaluate/Design (L5–L6); Q3–Q5: Recall

questions.explanation: Option B correctly implements 2:2:1: Q1–Q2 L1, Q3–Q4 L2, Q5 L3. Difficulty escalates appropriately for Beginners. A=all L1; C=all L3 (too hard for beginners); D=L5–L6 inappropriate for Beginner level.
questions.source: Anderson & Krathwohl. A Taxonomy for Learning. 2001.

table_of_contents.title: MQ-4 · Assessment Design & AI in Education
